subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Slope Field, Linear Approximation & Separation of Variables
stimulus: Consider the differential equation $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{2}\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}x\right)(y+7)$. Let $y = f(x)$ be the particular solution to the differential equation with the initial condition $f(1) = 2$. The function $f$ is defined for all real numbers.

parts.prompt: A portion of the slope field for the differential equation is given. Sketch the solution curve through the point $(1, 2)$.
parts.scoring_notes: All local maximum/minimum points on the solution curve must occur at horizontal slope segments. Curve must stay above $y = 0$ (since $f(x) > 0$).

scoring_points.description: Solution curve passes through $(1, 2)$, extends close to the left and right edges of the field, follows slope lines with no obvious conflicts, and $f(x) > 0$ is indicated throughout

required_elements: Solution curve passes through $(1, 2)$, extends close to the left
right edges of the field, follows slope lines with no obvious conflicts,

parts.prompt: Write an equation for the line tangent to the solution curve in part (a) at the point $(1, 2)$. Use the equation to approximate $f(0.8)$.
parts.scoring_notes: Slope at $(1,2)$: $\frac{dy}{dx}\big|_{(1,2)} = \frac{1}{2}\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)(2+7) = \frac{1}{2}(1)(9) = \frac{9}{2}$. Tangent line: $y = 2 + \frac{9}{2}(x-1)$. Approximation: $f(0.8) \approx 2 + \frac{9}{2}(0.8-1) = 2 - 0.9 = 1.1$. A response of only the expression $2 + \frac{9}{2}(0.8-1)$ earns P2 but not P1.

scoring_points.description: Correct tangent line equation at $(1, 2)$ with slope $\frac{9}{2}$

parts.prompt: It is known that $f''(x) > 0$ for $-1 \le x \le 1$. Is the approximation found in part (b) an overestimate or an underestimate for $f(0.8)$? Give a reason for your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Reason must include $f''(x) > 0$, $f'(x)$ is increasing, or $f(x)$ is concave up. Must conclude 'underestimate.'

scoring_points.description: Underestimate; because $f''(x) > 0$ on $[-1,1]$, the graph of $f$ is concave up, so the tangent line lies below the graph and the approximation is an underestimate

required_elements: Underestimate; because $f''(x) > 0$ on $[-1,1]$, the graph of $f$ is concave up, so the tangent line lies below the graph

parts.prompt: Use separation of variables to find $y = f(x)$, the particular solution to the differential equation $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{2}\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}x\right)(y+7)$ with the initial condition $f(1) = 2$.
parts.scoring_notes: No separation = 0/5. No constant of integration = at most 3/5. Must earn separation and at least one antiderivative point to earn constant point. Separation: $\frac{dy}{y+7} = \frac{1}{2}\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}x\right)dx$. Left antiderivative: $\ln|y+7|$. Right antiderivative: $-\frac{1}{\pi}\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}x\right) + C$. Initial condition at $(1,2)$: $\ln(9) = -\frac{1}{\pi}\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) + C = 0 + C$, so $C = \ln 9$. Solving: $y + 7 = 9e^{-(1/\pi)\cos(\pi x/2)}$, so $y = 9e^{-\frac{1}{\pi}\cos\left(\frac{\pi x}{2}\right)} - 7$.

scoring_points.description: Correct antiderivative of right side: $-\frac{1}{\pi}\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}x\right) + C$
common_traps: Forgetting to divide by $\frac{\pi}{2}$ when integrating $\sin(\frac{\pi}{2}x)$

scoring_points.description: Uses initial condition $f(1) = 2$ to find constant of integration
